data.title: Broadcom Is Threatening AMD's Data Center Opportunity
data.description:
Real men have fabs. - AMD founder Jerry Sanders.

For Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD), losing its fabs that its founder cherished so much appears to have been an enormous blessing in disguise. Ironically, the company now has no fabs and is led by brilliant CEO Lisa Su. It is worth mentioning that, with deep computer science and electrical engineer knowledge, including three MIT degrees, she is probably more talented and capable than any of the recent CEOs at its main competitor Intel (INTC). The amount of shareholder value created by AMD in the past ten years is jaw-dropping, as is the value destruction that has taken place at Intel.

All of this to say that AMD has benefited from its rivals’ missteps, a capable and visionary CEO, but also from the forced decision to give up its fabs. Having to let go of its chip manufacturing operations because of its precarious financial position at the time was perhaps the best thing that could have happened to AMD. It then was forced to search for a manufacturing partner in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, aka TSMC (TSM). This in turn meant that it was able to free-ride on TSMC's cutting-edge manufacturing capabilities and deliver more advanced silicon without having to pay for all the necessary R&D and CapEx required.

The technology development cost was therefore spread between the multiple TSMC customers, and they have been sharing the rewards in the form of privileged access to cutting-edge nodes. This has meant that for the same design, AMD got better performance and energy efficiency compared to Intel.

Unfortunately for AMD, this long-term tailwind could be coming to an end. Intel has started using TSMC as a foundry for certain products such as the Lunar Lake processor, also known as the Core Ultra 200V series, which is being fully manufactured by TSMC. Further complicating things for AMD, Broadcom (AVGO) is reportedly considering a bid for Intel's chip design business, letting someone else take the fabs. A new fab-less Intel as part of Broadcom would free the business to fully take advantage of working with TSMC, and erase AMD's manufacturing competitive advantage of working with TSMC.

Weakening Competitive Moat

Broadcom is spoiling AMD's party in additional ways, including helping Nvidia's (NVDA) customers design their own artificial intelligence (AI) chips. Many AMD investors were hoping AMD GPUs would be the Pepsi (PEP) to Nvidia's Coca-Cola (KO). Unfortunately for AMD investors, it is increasingly looking like it will be a similar situation to Starbucks (SBUX) where the main competition is homemade coffee.

In a recent Bloomberg interview, Lisa Su sounds very confident for the most part. However, she struggled to come up with a good reply when Emily Chang asks her if the fact that many of her customers are now designing their own AI chips is a major threat for AMD. Broadcom has been helping many of these customers design their own chips. Even the likes of OpenAI and ByteDance (BDNCE) are talking to Broadcom to get help designing their own proprietary AI chips. The reason that they are partnering with Broadcom is because of their deep chip design knowledge and vast intellectual property. Broadcom has one of the broadest intellectual property portfolios in the industry, with roughly 21,000 patents.

Part of AMD's moat also rests on its x86 license, which it was able to obtain from Intel when IBM (IBM) said they required at least two CPU suppliers. Intel reluctantly licensed x86 to AMD, but the architecture is now becoming less relevant as mobile phones and tablets tend to use Arm Holdings' (ARM) architecture, with Apple (AAPL) even switching its Macs to an ARM-based architecture recently. Unlike with x86, AMD does not have any major competitive advantage when using ARM's architecture versus rivals like Qualcomm (QCOM) and MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF). Reportedly, there are more than 60 laptop designs from OEMs currently in production or development based on the Snapdragon X series. Microsoft hedged its x86 dependence by developing a Windows version for ARM devices, and professional reviews for Windows laptops using ARM-based CPUs like Qualcomm's Snapdragon X have a stark warning for AMD:

This is the fiercest Microsoft has been able to compete with MacBooks in price, performance, and battery life, and while Qualcomm’s Snapdragon chips don’t outright beat Apple’s M3 chip (with an eight-core CPU and 10-core GPU) in every single one of our benchmarks, they could make Intel and AMD scramble to catch up to another competitor — this time, on their home turf.

Financials

Broadcom's strategy appears to be paying off, as its revenue has inflected higher while AMD's appears to be decelerating. AMD's $25.8 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2024 was only 14% higher compared to the previous year, even though there was an improvement in the fourth quarter to a ~24% growth rate. This is still significantly slower compared to Broadcom's current growth rate, and Broadcom is also delivering higher gross margins.

AMD delivered $1.0 in GAAP earnings per share (EPS) during fiscal year 2024, and $3.31 in Non-GAAP EPS. We would caution against relying too much on the Non-GAAP figure, given that a major reason for the discrepancy is due to $0.86 in stock-based compensation (“SBC”), which we consider to be a very real expense even if it is non-cash. Looking at the different business segments, Data center and Client continue to perform well, both growing significantly year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter. Gaming is showing signs of bottoming out, as it was significantly down year-over-year, but up quarter-over-quarter. Meanwhile, the Embedded business continues to show poor performance, although the decline rate appears to be moderating.

Stock-based Compensation

For a company with a GAAP net profit margin of ~6%, we believe its stock-based compensation expense of roughly 4.4% of quarterly revenue to be excessive. Since 2022 the gross amount of stock-based compensation has more than tripled from ~$400 million to close to $1.4 billion in the trailing twelve months.

Over the past ten years, average diluted shares outstanding have roughly doubled. Ironically, many companies add-back SBC arguing that it is a non-cash expense, but then spend billions repurchasing shares. This is the case with AMD, announcing in February 2022 an $8 billion share repurchase authorization, yet this has barely been able to offset the impact of stock-based compensation, as can be seen in the graph below.

Danger of Leveraged AMD ETFs

Still, we agree that AMD is a well run technology company and would not bet against it, and can understand why some investors continue to invest. Where we see enormous risk is with the leveraged ETFs on AMD's daily returns, such as the GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF (AMDL). Long-term investors should pay attention to the volatility drag effect, which means that capital can be destroyed even if the underlying shares deliver a positive return. Below is a hypothetical example showing how AMD shares could end up delivering a slight positive return after a few days of high volatility, while the leveraged ETF would have lost money.

Day 1 (-10%) Day 2 (-20%) Day 3 (+20%) Day 4 (+20%)
AMD 0.9 0.72 0.86 1.03
AMD 2x Long ETF 0.8 0.48 0.67 0.94

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as AMDL. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Outlook

AMD's guidance for the first quarter of fiscal year 2025 is for revenue of roughly $7.1 billion, which at the mid-point would represent growth of ~30% compared to the first quarter of fiscal year 2024. While this is an impressive growth rate, it is about a third of Nvidia's recent growth rate, meaning that the company is losing market share. Looking further ahead, we believe that growth is likely to further decelerate as many current customers begin transitioning to their proprietary solutions.

Valuation

We find AMD's valuation difficult to justify, with a GAAP Price/Earnings multiple in the triple digits, an EV/EBITDA of ~34x, and a market cap exceeding 7 times its revenue.

Compared to the information technology sector, AMD appears expensive on most valuation metrics when using the GAAP version. For instance, the forward Price/Earnings multiple is almost 50% higher compared to the sector median. Other relevant valuation metrics, such as the forward EV/Sales ratio, is roughly 70% above the sector median. With FY2024 revenue growth of only 14%, and a single-digit GAAP net profit margin, it is difficult to justify such a wide premium.

Investors should be aware that the current valuation for most of the companies involved with AI are discounting enormous growth long into the future, and prices could decline significantly even with minor growth disappointments. AMD only appears reasonably valued when compared to peers like ARM.

Risks

The biggest risk we see for AMD currently is the stretched valuation, even after shares have retreated from previous highs. The company is still growing revenue at a fast pace, but at a lower rate than competitors like Nvidia and Broadcom. Its Gaming and Embedded segments are also delivering relatively poor performance, and competition will probably intensify in its Client segment with ARM-based laptops.

In the medium to long term, the biggest risk we see for AMD is that many of its current customers, with the help of Broadcom and other chip specialists, switch to proprietary devices. We also see a significant risk to AMD should Broadcom acquire the design part of Intel, as it could move production to TSMC and eliminate one of AMD's advantages.

Conclusion

Broadcom is helping AMD clients develop their custom AI chips, which could impact the company's best performing business segment. The company's valuation is very high, especially when stock-based compensation is considered. While the company has delivered strong growth, and continues growing at a rapid pace, we see multiple risks. Many of them are the result of Broadcom, and AMD customers' desire to move to their proprietary designs, as well as the risk that Broadcom could buy Intel's chip design business and move manufacturing to TSMC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ON Holding: A Decent Buy Signal After The Recent Pullback For This Swiss Sportswear Company
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The stock of ON Holding AG (NYSE:ONON) is quite new on the New York Stock Exchange, going public on September 15th 2021, with an opening price of $35.40.

Since then, the movement of the price has been marked by two distinct phases: Low volume liquidations during the first 18 months, which led ONON to lose more than 50% of its value, effectively shaking out impatient short-term speculators, and strong buying interest in the following period up to today, with significantly increased trading volumes and the stock recording gains of +140% (+52% from the IPO), reaching historic highs.

The prevailing two-year uptrend appears very healthy, especially when considering the consistently strong upward movement of key financial metrics, which we will examine below, and I find it likely that this trend will continue.

The recent -21% pullback presents a repositioning opportunity, with an initial target of $74 representing a +45% potential gain.

Company Overview

On Holding AG is a Swiss company operating in the athletic apparel industry, with a focus on running shoes and sportswear. Despite being relatively “young” (founded in 2010), it quickly gained popularity by introducing an innovative technology (CloudTec) while at the same time, its differentiated business model (using direct-to-consumer DTC channels and targeting premium consumers willing to pay for high-performance footwear, unlike budget-friendly competitors) allowed it to stand out among industry giants like Nike and Adidas.

The team of Martin Hoffman and Marc Maurer has achieved significant market share growth through controlled, multi-channel expansion, with a strong focus on the active sports community. Equally impressive is the expansion of its geographic footprint, primarily driven by its online platform, where the number of members has skyrocketed (multiplying ninefold since its listing on the NYSE).

Finally, ON’s products are available in all major existing retail networks (footlocker, Dick’s, bloomingdale’s, Hype etc.) confirming their widespread acceptance by consumers worldwide.

The above factors have led the Company to steady growth over the years, demonstrating its strong foundation and long-term potential for stability and further evolution.

Solid financials, high profit margins

Before diving into a technical analysis of ONON’s stock, let’s first take a look at its financial position, focusing on three key aspects.

What stands out to me the most is its remarkable ability to grow both in terms of revenue and earnings, despite operating in an intensely competitive industry, facing well-established giants like Nike (NYSE:NKE) and Adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY).

Attempting a direct comparison with these Companies ($109 billion vs $17 billion) would be futile, but evaluating ON individually, I see a fundamentally strong company with solid characteristics and a strong likelihood of continuing to gain market share while improving its financials, further proof of the broad acceptance of its products.

The figures are impressive and ONON appears to outperform the entire sector in revenue, EBITDA and EPS growth, even when compared to the two industry giants.

I expect ONON to continue gaining market share, which suggests that its stock has significant upside potential in the medium to long-term. This assumption is based primarily on its (so far) successful business model and its competitive advantages. My focus is on three main points:

  • Omnichannel Distribution Strategy - leveraging direct-to-consumer DTC channels, premium retail partnerships and digital sales.
  • Premium Pricing Strategy- targeting premium consumers willing to pay for high performance footwear, unlike budget-friendly competitors.
  • Innovative Footwear Technology- including CloudTec technology, Speedboard innovation and the CyclonlLine.

In November 2024, ON reached record sales and profit margins and raised its FY2024 guidance, expecting sales growth (as of March 4) of at least 32% and a gross profit margin increase of 100bps.

The second key point worth noting is the efficient way ONON converts strong revenue into income, as its net income margin is estimated to be just below 6%, compared to the sector’s weighted average of 4.5%.

This figure alone is meaningful, but more importantly, it reinforces my confidence that the expected growth we discussed earlier will also translate into strong profitability. This is why I believe the stock's upward momentum is unlikely to slow down anytime soon.

The third key point worth highlighting is ON’s cash flow performance. Firstly, its operating cash flow has seen remarkable improvement, currently estimated at $547 million, compared to a five-year average of $62 million (having been negative until mid-2023). Even more striking is its exceptionally high LFCF Margin of 15.45% (net cash flow available for distribution after covering interest payments and tax obligations), which is three times higher than the average of the sector.

Looking the Company as a whole, it is clear that ONON is a highly promising case. While it lacks the long-standing track record of more established industry players, the available data is undeniably impressive and its product is objectively strong.

Risks: High valuation, strong competition

Now let’s look at the downsides. Unsurprisingly, ONON’s valuation is high as the stock trades near its all-time highs, at 114 times its earnings and 76 times its FWD earnings. This presents a significant challenge both when compared to the broader sector (where ONON trades at five times the average of the sector) and when looking at other valuation metrics, such as P/CF (30x TTM, 73x FWD), despite the Company’s exceptionally strong performance in these areas.

This elevated valuation acts as a deterrent for potential investors, who may hesitate to buy at such a high premium, even after the recent 21% pullback, which naturally reduced the P multiple. It is also important to remember that, despite the Company’s strong growth, this high valuation could indicate that market expectations are already priced in to some extent.

Additionally, the most significant risk is ONON’s future. As discussed earlier, the company is currently fully focused on growth (and is executing exceptionally well), aggressively capturing market share from well-established competitors. For now, it is succeeding due to the product differentiation we have highlighted, but maintaining this trajectory in the long run will be a key challenge.

There is still one key question: What triggered the recent -21% pullback? A primary explanation is the anticipation and eventual downgrade of ONON by Goldman Sachs, which revised its rating on On Holding AG from “Buy” to “Neutral”, while maintaining a $57 price target. This decision was based on concerns over increasing competition in the athletic footwear market and signs of slowing growth in the U.S.

Therefore, for someone to invest at this point, they would need to be convinced (as I am) that these factors will not have a lasting impact on the stock, preventing further selling pressure.

It is also worth noting that while the company has disappointed in the last two quarters, it traditionally delivers strong positive surprises in Q1, driven by seasonal trends.

Stock Overview: Strong upward momentum and an interesting buy signal

The stock is clearly in an uptrend. The investment community has already begun to believe that this “young” company will continue gaining market share, while the strong profit margins we analyzed earlier further reinforce its position.

The recent pullback from all-time highs has, at last for now, found support at $51, a level from which I expect a strong upward reaction in the short term. If momentum holds, this could pave the way for a new rally towards $74, setting fresh all-time highs. This target aligns with the upper boundary of the current bullish movement.

Momentum remains very strong and the +45% upside potential to my target is attractive enough for me to take the trade, despite the elevated valuation. After all, looking at the stock’s behavior over the past few years, pullbacks have been frequent and each has proven valuable in the bigger picture.

Its liquidity is more than sufficient, as an average of 1% of shares outstanding are traded daily, with a very tight bid-ask spread of approximately $0.1.

Conclusion

ONON is a highly promising and relatively young $17 billion company in the footwear industry, with strong and healthy financials.

The widespread consumer acceptance of its products and its business model justify its successful movement so far.

Its stock has been following a high-quality uptrend, with strong prospects for continuation, potentially reaching new all-time highs.

The recent pullback has created a repositioning opportunity, with an initial upside potential of 45%.

data.title: ETW: Collecting A Global Income Stream But With A Caveat
data.description:
Overview

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (NYSE:ETW) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of equities and included option writing strategy. The fund's inclusion of option writing means that there is a clear focus on income generation. Therefore, ETW should only be utilized by investors that are intentionally seeking a high level of distribution income and aren't necessarily looking to capture the best possible total return. One of the primary appeals of ETW is that the fund offers a high dividend yield of 9.2% and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis.

ETW has a public inception dating back to 2005, which means that we have over two decades worth of performance data to reference. The fund has a reasonable management fee of 1% but the total expense ratio is 1.11% when including miscellaneous expenses. Looking at the performance of ETW over the last twelve months, we can see that the price has increased by about 8.6%. When including distributions, the total return jumps up above 20.3% during the same period. While ETW has a strong performance over 2024, I wanted to provide some insights to whether or not the fund will be able to provide similar returns going forward.

Another interest aspect of ETW is that the fund maintains a bit of global exposure, as stated in the name. ETW can be an efficient fund to diversify your income away from US markets. Additionally, the fund aims to pay distributions out in a tax efficient manner, making ETW able to be utilized outside of tax-advantaged accounts. While the focus on income comes with sole risks and tradeoffs that investors should be aware of, I think it's a solid fund that provides diversified exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. So let's first start by taking a look at the fund's portfolio strategy and investment methodology.

Fund Strategy

According to ETW's most recent fact sheet, net assets amount to a little over $1B and are spread across a variety of sectors and regions. Although ETW does provide some global exposure, approximately 57.78% of assets are still based within North America. The fund provides a near 30% exposure to European assets and a 12.5% exposure to Asian Pacific assets. We can also see that 27.59% of the fund is comprised of information technology investments. This is followed by exposure to consumer discretionary and financial investments, accounting for 13.98% and 12.07% respectively.

The combination of the top three sectors make up over 56% of net assets within ETW. Although the fund is diversified across other sectors, it's safe to assume that the daily price swings within these top three sectors will have the greatest influence over the price movements ETW experiences.

In terms of top holdings, we can see that ETW grants immediate diversification into some of the most recognizable large cap names in the world. Net assets are spread across 279 different equities. Apple (AAPL) is the largest individual position making up 5.24% of assets. This is followed by exposure to NVIDIA (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT), making up 4.64% and 4.48% respectively. The first you may notice is that plenty of the top ten holdings here have experienced massive double digit growth over the last five year period. So why hasn't ETW seen a similar type of movement?

The lack of upside price growth can be attributed to ETW's inclusion of an option writing strategy. According to the fund's commentary, it seems like ETW uses indexed ATM (at-the-money) call options to generate additional income against the underlying equities. The issue is that ATM options generally have strike prices that are close to the current market price of the underlying asset, which severely caps the upside price appreciation that can be experienced. This is why I want to put emphasis on the fact that ETW should only be utilized if you seek income.

To help demonstrate how this works, let's imagine that ETW holds a position in an asset that is priced at $100 per share. ETW's ATM approach means that it will deploy a call option and select a strike price that is near the current market price. In this instance, let's just say that ETW selected a strike price of $102 per share. If the underlying asset has a great quarterly earnings and the price shoots up to $105 per share, ETW would only capture the upside growth up to the strike price, effectively sacrificing everything above the $102 level.

Valuation & Performance

Since ETW operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the net assets within. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 8.93%. Initiating a position within a closed end fund while it trades at a discount can be beneficial for long term holders. For reference, the price has traded at an average discount to NAV of 7.88% over the last three year period, indicating that the current discount is more attractive than the average. We can see that the price briefly touched the premium to NAV territory several times over the last ten years, usually driven by a strong bull market.

When closed end funds trade at a discount to NAV, it could be an indication of some sort of weakness. However, I took a look at the most recent released reporting to see if there was anything unusual. The latest report available is the semi-annual report. Looking at performance for the first six months of 2024, we can see that net investment income amounted to $0.104 while net realized gains landed at $0.530 per share.

Looking back over prior years, net investment income levels have consistently been low when compared to net realized gains. Therefore, it seems like ETW is reliant on bull markets to fuel consistent NAV growth over time. NAV growth seems to align with the general conditions of the market; a bullish market movement led to NAV growth while a bearish market led to NAV loss. For reference, here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $10.47
  • 2020: $10.30
  • 2021: $10.93
  • 2022: $8.38
  • 2023: $9.05
  • 2024 (semi-annual): $9.31

As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture net realized gains when the market is moving downward. So I would ideally like to see net investment income levels improve over time to take away from the reliance on net realized gains to fund NAV growth. If net investment income levels are improved, it would increase the rate at which the NAV is able to grow over time, after paying out distributions.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.0664 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.2%. If we multiply this monthly payout by 12, we get an estimated yearly distribution of $0.7968 per share. Referring back to the semi annual report, ETW has historically been able to cover this amount through a combination of net investment income and net realized gains. However, we can see that the dividend history over the last decade has trended downward and long term holders may have seen shrinking income if they didn't continuously add new capital or reinvest distributions. The lack of dividend consistency and growth can be attributed to the reliance on net realized gains.

For instance, the dividend was most recently cut back in 2022. This is a direct result of the fund's underperformance as markets were in a downward trend over the entirety of 2022. Therefore, investors seeking the most reliable source of dividend income may need to look elsewhere. I personally will remain on the sidelines as well because I tend to prefer funds that have a history of either maintaining consistent payouts or growing dividends over a long period of time.

Therefore, the sustainability of the distribution will always be vulnerable to downward markets. The fund is forced to cut distributions when markets decline to preserve the NAV. If the fund didn't cut distributions while it wasn't actively earning enough to support those distributions, it would cause the NAV to be heavily impacted.

However, one of the primary benefits that ETW aims for is to distribution income to shareholders in a tax efficient manner. Looking at the prior distributions over the last twelve months, it looks like the majority of each monthly distribution is comprised of the combination of long term capital and return of capital. Long term capital gains are taxed at much more favorable rates than distributions funded by net investment income.

Additionally, return of capital distributions generally have more favorable tax treatment. This is because the distributions themselves aren't actually considered to be income. Instead, return of capital distributions typically reduce the shareholders' cost basis and the taxes are deferred until the time of sale. The emphasis on using long term gains and return of capital means that ETW could efficiently be utilized within a regular taxable brokerage account, increasing its overall flexibility.

Risk Profile

The most obvious risk would be underperformance in relation to the market indexes. Just as a comparison, I wanted to demonstrate how much ETW underperforms the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) from both a price and total return perspective. We can see how the option strategy severely limits the price upside, leading to ETW missing out on the few bull runs that we've seen over the last decade. For comparison, ETW's price has fallen by 26%, while SPY is up by 190% and QQQ has increased by over 402%. Obviously this isn't the best side by side comparison since these funds have different strategies and a different underlying equity focus. However, even with ETW's much higher dividend yield, it still underperforms from a total return standpoint as well.

Additionally, the emphasis on income makes ETW even less resilient than SPY during market declines. The continued high rate of distributions will erode the fund's NAV more rapidly during market declines and this can be reinforced by simply looking at the performance over the course of 2022. The markets were in a continuous decline for the entire year and despite ETW's much higher dividend yield, the fund still underperformed and had a greater loss than SPY. So I will continue to put emphasis on the fact that this fund is not suitable for investors that aim to achieve a high level of total return. If you are a younger investor and don't necessarily have the need for income at this stage in your journey, I wouldn't suggest holding ETW.

Takeaway

In conclusion, ETW prioritizes income generation from its underlying portfolio of equities. Despite having exposure to some of the best performing large cap companies in the world, ETW will continue to underperform these more traditional assets and indexes because of the option strategy that limits the upside price appreciation. Since the fund also aims to distribution its income in a tax efficient manner, the fund is heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth while simultaneously covering the bulk of the distributions. While the fund has still provided a positive total return over the last decade, I do not think that ETW is a suitable choice for most investors.

If you are seeking the best possible total return, ETW is not for you. Additionally, the fund has a history of decreasing its dividend payments whenever the market slides. If you are an income investor, I think the consistent cuts defeat the purpose of holding an asset that is supposed to continuously pay you a high rate of income. For this reason, I only mark ETW as a hold because of the tax efficient aspect and global exposure that it can provide.

data.title: Bausch Health Q4 Earnings Recap (Reiterating Buy Rating)
data.description:
In the last quarter, after Bausch Health Companies (NYSE:BHC) posted results, shares entered a steady downtrend. Last week, BHC stock found support at around $6.30. On Thursday, Feb. 19, the out-of-favor firm unexpectedly posted earnings and revenue that beat consensus estimates. Shares are up by over 10% at the time of writing.

In my two previous articles, I rated Bausch Health a stock to buy. The stock returned nearly 26% since last July 2024 and lost around 3% since May. What should investors consider after the fourth-quarter earnings report?

Q4 Results

BHC reported Q4 GAAP earnings per share of $0.25. Revenue increased by 6.2% Y/Y to $2.56 billion. It issued full-year revenue growth of 2.0% to 6.0% and adjusted EBITDA growth of 3.0% to 7.0%.

BHC highlighted achieving seven straight quarters of Y/Y growth in revenue and adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA. Revenue increased by 10% for the year, when excluding foreign exchange ($70 million), acquisitions ($293 million), and divestitures and discontinuations ($77 million). In Q4, revenue increased in all segments except International and Diversified Products. However, excluding the impact of foreign exchange, the International segment revenue increased by 1%.

Overall, shareholders should welcome the consolidated positive net income of $93 million, compared to the $39 million net loss last year.

Upcoming Catalysts

BHC’s litigation around the Food and Drug Administration involving Norwich is a positive catalyst. Chief Executive Officer Thomas Appio said on the conference call that the FDA denied giving Norwich final approval. It concluded that Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (TEVA) did not forfeit its first filer status. Norwich sued the FDA, requesting the agency to grant final approval of its ANDA.

BHC believes that the FDA is correct in its determination of Teva remaining the first filer.

The Norwich case is a positive catalyst since it has an impact on the FDA approval of a generic Xifaxan. With a 30-month stay still applying, BHC may focus on the success of this drug. CEO Appio said that it is prepared to go through the Medicare renegotiation list that comes into effect in 2027. BHC is in a strong position with the drug since it achieves cost savings and benefits.

The CEO said, “In terms of when you look at Xifaxan on the OHE indication, which is more than 70% of the business, the amount of cost savings regarding hospitalizations is -- it drives a big benefit.”

Bausch & Lomb’s discontinuation of the sale process on Feb. 6 is another positive development. B+L said that “after engagement with potential buyers, that process is complete, and will not result in a transaction at this time.” The company is better off realizing higher shareholder returns than selling itself to private banks at a discount. BHC’s EVP and CFO Jean-Jacques Charhon said on the call that it will seek value creation through its BLCO equity stake.

While the CFO has many options available, he did not provide many details. Using the BLCO equity stake as collateral for financing is one of many options. In addition, he set a three-year timeframe for the asset monetization. The timing will depend on BHC’s covenants and obligations related to the debt it plans to raise.

2025 Revenue Growth Catalysts

The CFO expects that Salix, Xifaxan, and Solta will contribute the most to BHC’s growth this year. BHC stock is rallying after the earnings report on expectations that the growth momentum will continue. The firm requires cash flow growth to cover payments for its interest on debt. Fortunately, the debt obligation peaks in 2028. This will give BHC the time it needs to accelerate its long-term growth.

Risks

The risk of U.S. tariffs on trade partners is on most investors’ minds. For BHC, CFO Charhon said that the assumed 25% tariffs would have an impact of less than $50 million in cash flow for the full year.

The Xifaxan price negotiation is another risk. The firm may not get the prices that it wants, hurting its cash flow forecast after January 2027.

Takeaway

BHC stock is among the drug manufacturers that are out of favor. Bigger firms like Merck (MRK), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Teva Pharmaceutical also traded lower in recent weeks.

The over 10% rally in BHC stock could fade if buying volume weakens in the coming days. For long-term investors uninterested in trading the stock’s volatility, the strong Q4 report is positive news. It reaffirms my “Buy” rating on the company.

data.title: Corporación América Airports: Accelerating Traffic Growth To Power Next Leg Higher
data.description:
Corporación América Airports S.A. (NYSE:CAAP) is one of the world's leading private airport concession holders. It operates 52 airports spanning South America, Europe, and Eurasia.

The company got its start in 1998 when it won the AA2000 concession in Argentina, which gave it the right to manage most of Argentina's leading airports. It controls these airports, including both major Buenos Aires ones, through 2038. It has also since added airport operations in Brazil, Italy, Armenia, Ecuador, and Uruguay. It's also seeking potential expansion opportunities in additional markets, such as Nigeria and Angola.

I have been a proponent of CAAP stock dating back to 2020 when I highlighted that its liquidity position was better than the market was giving it credit for, thus making it likely to survive the global travel shutdown. CAAP stock has since rallied from $2/share at that point to almost $20 per share today.

Even after the huge move, however, I remain convinced that the stock remains substantially undervalued. Here's why I see CAAP stock as a strong buy today.

Strong Traffic Growth And Increased Profitability

I recently highlighted how Mexico's Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte, S.A.B. de C.V. (OMAB) has seen its traffic inflect sharply higher over the past quarter. As recently as November 2024, OMAB had been comping negative traffic year-over-year, but that has now surged to 9.9%-plus as of January 2025. It was understandable to think that this dynamic was somewhat unique to the Mexican market as Mexico's leading carrier, Controladora Vuela Compañía de Aviación, S.A.B. de C.V. (VLRS), was hit especially hard by the Pratt & Whitney engine defects.

However, CAAP is now reporting a similarly vigorous turn in its traffic data. Here's a table showing the company's air traffic by country in January 2025 as compared to January 2024:

Jan'25

Jan'24

% Change

Passenger Traffic (thousands)

Argentina

4,218

3,738

12.8%

Italy

485

459

5.6%

Brazil

1,283

1,497

-14.3%

Uruguay

243

232

4.7%

Ecuador

373

345

8.1%

Armenia

393

368

6.7%

TOTAL

6,995

6,640

5.3%

Source - Corporate press release. Note that Brazil was down because CAAP's Natal airport concession ended in 2024.

I highlight the country-by-country traffic data to show that CAAP is now reporting favorable results in all markets except Brazil, which is unique because it ceased operating the Natal Airport in February 2024. Excluding the Natal lease effect, CAAP's overall traffic grew 9.5% year-over-year. This was up significantly on a sequential basis from the 6.6% increase noted in December and the 0.2% decline that CAAP posted in November, respectively.

Making matters even better, CAAP reported a 13.9% growth rate in international traffic for January, including a stunning 22% rise in international traffic in its Argentine market.

This is vital to profitability as international traffic has higher revenue per passenger than domestic. That's true in general for airport operators as international passengers have a higher propensity to spend on food, retail, gifts and ancillary services such as hotel and car rental. It's specifically even more true for CAAP due to its fee structure in Argentina.

CAAP charges a low rate, typically equal to a couple bucks, for each departing passenger on Argentine domestic flights, and the rate is set in Argentine Pesos, meaning it's subject to dramatic FX volatility. For international passengers departing Argentina, however, CAAP gets to charge a fixed $57 rate, set in U.S. dollars. This is a large part of CAAP's appeal in that it gives investors exposure to the Argentine economic recovery while still having guaranteed U.S. dollar-based revenues that are insulated from potential Peso devaluation.

The Airport Operator's Shares Remain Sharply Undervalued

It's easy to look at a stock that has already enjoyed a huge rally and think that it must be reaching a full valuation.

In some cases, however, fundamentals keep up with the stock price, and shares remain cheap even after a big run. CAAP stock is one such case.

CAAP completed its IPO while trading around 11x EV/EBITDA back in 2018 and shares were trading at or above 8x up until the Argentine economy plummeted in 2019.

CAAP stock was selling for as low as 5x EV/EBITDA during the pandemic, such as in 2022. Surprisingly enough, it remains quite close to its historical low valuation:

How has CAAP stock gone up so dramatically while the EV/EBITDA ratio has remained below 7x?

That's because Corporación America Airports was able to slash its expenses dramatically during the pandemic, and this gave it a better operational footing as the world economy reopened. Argentina, in particular, is known for higher labor costs and elevated overhead compared to other LatAm markets, so the pandemic was a unique opportunity to reduce costs that likely would otherwise have remained bloated.

Add it all up and even though overall traffic is only modestly above 2019 levels, CAAP's EBITDA is already close to 50% higher now than it was at pre-pandemic times:

And now with passenger traffic seemingly on course for a double-digit growth year in 2025, we could be set for a serious upward move in the firm's EBITDA generation capacity this year.

If we suppose that CAAP is worth 10x EV/EBITDA, roughly on par with where it traded post-IPO in 2018, how would that work out? I'd note that in 2018, Argentina had a conservative government, under President Macri, but one which was more moderate in nature than the current Javier Milei-led libertarian government. Under Macri and with a less favorable operating cost structure, CAAP was able to garner a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple.

Adding three turns of EBITDA to CAAP's valuation would require increasing the enterprise value by about $1.8 billion. Meanwhile, CAAP has 161 million shares of stock outstanding. This implies that CAAP stock needs to trade up by about $11/share, giving us a price target of $31 to get to a still modest 10x EV/EBITDA multiple. In the event that CAAP stock trades up to 12x EBITDA, this would suggest a fair value for CAAP stock of about $38 per share.

Again, that's using the firm's current EBITDA. Figure in double-digit traffic growth this year, and we could easily be printing closer to $700 million in annual EBITDA going forward, which would move my fair value targets for CAAP stock into the $40s.

I've spent most of the valuation discussion on EV/EBITDA as it's an industry standard for airport companies. Depreciation is a sizable cost here, and due to the very different cost bases for various airport contracts around the world, GAAP earnings can be less than apples-to-apples comparison between different airport operators.

That said, CAAP stock also trades incredibly cheap on a P/E basis as well. The company has earned $2.16 over the past 12 months, which puts the stock at less than 10x trailing earnings. Earnings have surged dramatically since 2022 and are now far beyond the company's pre-pandemic earnings power.

The Seeking Alpha earnings consensus only has two analysts in it, as CAAP is not a widely followed stock. That said, I'd suggest this set of estimates is conservative:

The down year-over-year estimate for 2024 implies a poor Q4 which seems improbable given the favorable traffic trends we've seen in recent months. And given said traffic growth, it's quite likely that CAAP can exceed $2/share in earnings in 2025 as well. EPS figures can be fairly volatile here due to FX fluctuations and also CAAP's costs associated with expanding its airports and doing work for bidding on potential new concessions.

That said, even on analyst projections shown above, CAAP stock is quite cheap on an earnings basis, especially at 7.9x projected 2026 earnings. And from my perspective, these numbers are a touch too low, and we could easily see them overshoot on the upside.

CAAP Stock Verdict

Let's briefly touch on the positive macroeconomic factors for Corporación America Airports. The travel industry remains strong, and that's particularly true in Latin American markets where the company derives the majority of its revenues.

Argentina is a particularly compelling market right now due to its President, Javier Milei. Milei was the former chief economist at Corporación America, the parent company of the airport operator. Not surprisingly, he has focused on aviation industry policy since becoming president and has taken aggressive measures to deregulate the Argentine airline industry and welcome foreign airline competition. These measures are starting to pay off, as CAAP's recent traffic data indicate.

That said, there's an argument for the idea that investor sentiment is reaching near-euphoric levels around the Argentine equity market and that some issuers, such as Argentine banks, have probably become overvalued. To that point, the Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (ARGT) is up 70% over the past 12 months and more than 200% over the past five years. I have tempered my enthusiasm for Argentine equities, as a whole, given the dramatic rise in valuations in that market over the past 18 months.

However, as I aimed to show in this article, CAAP stock remains compellingly cheap in its own right. And due to so much of its revenues being in U.S. dollars and Euros, CAAP is much less exposed to internal Argentine economic or currency fluctuations than most other Argentine-listed companies.

As such, regardless of your view on Argentina as a country or economy, CAAP stock remains a strong opportunity. If you're bullish on Argentina, and President Milei continues to make major economic reforms in that country, all the better. But even putting that aside, CAAP stock is trading at about 10x earnings and 7x EV/EBITDA today and should be set for double-digit growth in 2025. Shares shouldn't stay on sale below $20 for long given these facts.
